I've and George Turner took his pick.
I do not want to say anything about
the pretensions OF my fellow towns
man 15 years ago. He a like a
good many others who sought to
dodge between the constitution and
between the legislature. We fought
together for the tide lands. In my

the biggest fight on principle which

convention. According to George
Turner the railroad commission ques-

lature.'


Judge J. 2. Moore ought to know
the fate of his suggestions to the
to remember what a man of his
delegation said in explanation Of his
vote. He ought to know whether
George Turner In those days vas a
turner or whether he was as lavish
in his distinguished ability and mas
terful logic" in those days as he Is
said to be in these days of the year
of the Lord, 1904.


In those days George Turner could
turn between the turns Of the exist
ing convention and the prospects of
future legislation on the railway
commission question. Records show
thai he did not pass his W railway
commission bill In 1897. when he was
elected to the United States senate.
Fol once he turned from the legislat-
i've to the constitutional. He will
probably turn again.


The specific enactments to which

whether there was any distinguished
ability exercised In the lodging or
''masterful logic" in getting to the

Against making 1t unlawful for

stitutional convention. page 315.
Judge Moore says that Turner was
for the matter to be left for legis-
lative enactment.


Against making 1t unlawful for any
railroad to give any public official a
pass. That was also subject matter
for legislation in the eyes Of Judge
Turner.


When George Turner was In su-
preme control of both branches Of the
legislature of the state of Washing
ton and got himself elected to the
United States senate. did he consider
whether he was making mincemeat
for the public out Of legislature or
constitutional convention?
